summary: The helper chain behind the 0x140C4C9C8 login/session root is now split into concrete roles. The root owns an active 0x268-byte OTL request slot at +0x68, a pending-entry range at +0x70..+0x78, and string-like session/user material around +0x50/+0x5F. Teardown and explicit consumers drain outstanding work, reset or free the active request slot, and can build the session/close request before destruction completes.
confidence: medium-high for helper call graph, global-root consumers, active request-slot reset, vector drain, and session/close literals; medium for exact wait-helper semantics and vector element layout.

helpers.role: Coordinator that drains current root state and, when session/user fields are populated, builds a session/close OTL request.
evidence: Called by the root destructor at 0x14016A7D9 and by nine direct consumers that load RCX from global 0x140C4C9C8 immediately before the call.
Checks string-like state around root +0x50/+0x5F and the active request slot at root +0x68.
Can call 0x14062B0B0 before continuing, tying close/flush behavior to the same pending-work drain used by destruction.

Walks the pending-entry range from root +0x70 to root +0x78 with a 0x78-byte stride, waits through 0x14062CA70 with stack value 0x64 while entry state remains live, calls 0x140172E60, then rewinds root +0x78 back to root +0x70.

References session/close/%s/%s, ?sessionDuration=%d, and ?userHash=%s&sessionDuration=%d, allocates a 0x268-byte request object, initializes it through 0x14062B7B0, and submits it through 0x14062BCE0.

helpers.role: Drain helper for the active request slot and pending-entry range.
evidence: Called by the root destructor at 0x14016A7E1 with RCX copied from the destructor root, by 0x14062AB70, and by the session/open builder neighborhood at 0x14062A7BA.
If root +0x68 is non-null, loops on active request state and calls 0x14062CA70 with stack value 0x64 until the pending condition clears.
Calls 0x14062AF20 after the active request wait loop, so request-slot reset/free is centralized rather than duplicated.
Walks root +0x70..+0x78 with 0x78-byte entries, waits on live entry state, releases the range through 0x140172E60, and rewinds +0x78.
Called directly by a global-root consumer at 0x1400CE1ED with RCX loaded from 0x140C4C9C8 and internally by 0x14062B0B0 at 0x14062B0F5.
Reads root +0x68, uses nested request data at +0x80 in a 0x140031B10 formatting/copy helper path, then clears root +0x68 to zero.
If an active request was present, calls 0x14062B8C0 and frees the 0x268-byte request object through 0x1407B8478.
Falls into the same pending-entry range handling pattern around root +0x70/+0x78 after clearing the active slot.

helpers.role: Wait/deadline helper used by the request and entry drain loops.
evidence: Called from 0x14062AB70 and 0x14062B0B0 after callers place 0x64 into a stack local and pass that local by RCX.
Contains timebase constants 0x989680, 0x3B9ACA00, and 0x311CDAD160000, consistent with tick/nanosecond/deadline conversion rather than ordinary object cleanup.

field_map_delta.role: String-like session/user material with sentinel or length state checked before session/close construction.
Active 0x268-byte OTL request slot. It is checked by 0x14062AB70 and 0x14062B0B0, cleared by 0x14062AF20, and released by the root destructor if still present.
Pending-entry range start/end, walked with a 0x78-byte stride and rewound by the drain helpers.
Nested pointer or range bound read from the active request object by 0x14062AF20 before the request slot is cleared.

callsite_evidence: 0x14062AB70 has ten direct callsites; nine external callsites recover RCX from [0x140C4C9C8], and the destructor callsite is inside 0x14016A7D1.
0x14062B0B0 has three direct callsites: the root destructor, 0x14062AB70, and the session/open builder neighborhood at 0x14062A760.
0x14062AF20 has two direct callsites: one external global-root consumer and one internal call from 0x14062B0B0.
0x14062CA70 has three local direct callsites, all from the AB70/B0B0 drain loops with stack-local wait value 0x64.
opennxt_947_server_implications: Session close is coupled to async OTL request lifetime. A faithful OpenNXT 947 harness should expect close/flush behavior to wait for pending client-side request or entry state before fully releasing the login/session root.

The same 0x268 request lifecycle used by login/session builders is reused by the session/close path, so server traces should correlate session/open, session/close, and root +0x68 ownership rather than treating close as a standalone telemetry ping.
The wait helper suggests teardown may spin or yield around outstanding work. If OpenNXT tests assert immediate close emission or immediate cleanup, they may race the client-side drain behavior.
The +0x70/+0x78 vector/list is now a first-class mapping target because it bridges login/session root lifetime, pending async entries, and destructor safety.

reverser_tool_update.why_it_matters: Raw branch-target scans were reliable for cleanup thunks but scanning the full executable is noisy and expensive. The new --section option records section_filters metadata and limits branch scans to named executable sections such as .text.
validation: Added analysis support for section-filtered branch-target scans.
Added unit and CLI coverage for section filters.
Used the new option to generate the helper-chain branch-target artifact and refresh the previous lifecycle branch-target artifact.

next_targets: Decode 0x14062CA70 precisely enough to name whether 0x64 is a timeout, sleep duration, scheduler wait, or poll delay.
Map the 0x78-byte pending-entry layout reached through root +0x70/+0x78.
Resolve the indirect CALL R10 sites in 0x14062AB70 to identify the callback or vtable path used during close request construction.
Follow 0x14062BCE0 submit call arguments from the close path to label the request transport fields used by session/close.
